title: Photoelectric effect
summary: This lesson explores the photoelectric effect, a key phenomenon providing evidence for the quantum nature of light. It covers Einstein's photoelectric equation, the concepts of work function and threshold frequency, and the analysis of experimental data to determine Planck's constant.
learningObjectives: Recall and use Einstein's photoelectric equation $KE_{max} = hf - \Phi$.
Define and apply the concepts of work function ($\Phi$), threshold frequency ($f_0$), and photon.
Analyse graphical data of maximum kinetic energy versus frequency to determine the Planck constant and work function.
Explain why the wave theory of light fails to account for the observations of the photoelectric effect.

simpleExplanation.title: Light's Quantum Punch
simpleExplanation.summary: Light acts like a stream of tiny energy packets called photons. If a single photon has enough energy to knock an electron out of a metal surface, it does so instantly, with any leftover energy becoming the electron's kinetic energy.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine trying to buy a snack from a vending machine. The price of the snack is the 'work function' of the metal. The money you have is the energy of one photon. If you have exactly enough or more money, you can buy the snack (the electron is emitted). Any change you get back is the electron's kinetic energy. If you don't have enough money, you get nothing, no matter how many of your friends (higher intensity) also try with the same insufficient amount.
steps: Light delivers energy in discrete packets called photons, where a photon's energy is given by $E = hf$.
Electrons are bound to the metal surface with a minimum energy required for escape, known as the work function, $\Phi$.
If a photon's energy is greater than the work function ($hf > \Phi$), an electron is emitted. Its maximum kinetic energy is the leftover energy: $KE_{max} = hf - \Phi$.

Increasing the light's intensity (making it brighter) increases the number of photons arriving per second, thus increasing the rate of electron emission, but does not change the maximum kinetic energy of each electron.

flashcards.back: The emission of electrons (photoelectrons) from a metal surface when electromagnetic radiation of sufficiently high frequency is incident upon it.
A discrete packet or quantum of electromagnetic energy. Its energy is given by $E = hf$, where $h$ is the Planck constant and $f$ is the frequency of the radiation.
The minimum energy required to remove a single electron from the surface of a particular metal.
The minimum frequency of incident electromagnetic radiation required to cause photoelectric emission from a particular metal surface.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between work function $\Phi$ and threshold frequency $f_0$?
flashcards.back: The work function is the energy of a photon with the threshold frequency: $\Phi = hf_0$.
Maximum kinetic energy of an emitted electron is $KE_{max} = hf - \Phi$.

flashcards.front: How does increasing the intensity of the incident light affect the emitted photoelectrons?
flashcards.back: It increases the rate of emission of photoelectrons (more electrons per second), but has no effect on their maximum kinetic energy.

flashcards.front: How does increasing the frequency of the incident light (above $f_0$) affect the emitted photoelectrons?
flashcards.back: It increases the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons. The rate of emission is unaffected if the intensity (power per unit area) is kept constant.

flashcards.front: What happens if the frequency of the incident light is below the threshold frequency ($f < f_0$)?
flashcards.back: No photoelectric emission occurs, regardless of the intensity or duration of the illumination.

flashcards.front: In a graph of $KE_{max}$ against frequency $f$, what does the gradient represent?
flashcards.back: The gradient is equal to the Planck constant, $h$.

flashcards.front: In a graph of $KE_{max}$ against frequency $f$, what does the y-intercept represent?
flashcards.back: The y-intercept is equal to the negative of the work function, $-\Phi$.

flashcards.front: Why does the existence of a threshold frequency support the particle model of light?
flashcards.back: The wave model predicts that any frequency of light, if intense enough, should eventually provide enough energy to emit an electron. The particle model explains that one photon interacts with one electron, and if that single photon's energy ($hf$) is less than the work function ($\Phi$), no emission can occur.

faq.q: What is the difference between the work function and the threshold frequency?
faq.a: They are related concepts measuring the same property of a metal. The threshold frequency ($f_0$) is the minimum *frequency* of light needed for emission. The work function ($\Phi$) is the minimum *energy* needed for emission. They are linked by the equation $\Phi = hf_0$, where $h$ is the Planck constant.

faq.q: Why is it the *maximum* kinetic energy, $KE_{max}$?
faq.a: The work function, $\Phi$, is the *minimum* energy required to remove an electron. This applies to electrons on the very surface of the metal. Electrons deeper within the metal require more energy to escape because they may lose energy in collisions on their way out. Therefore, the photoelectrons have a range of kinetic energies from zero up to a maximum value, $KE_{max}$.

faq.q: If a photon has exactly the work function energy, is an electron emitted?
faq.a: Yes. If $hf = \Phi$, the electron is just liberated from the surface but has zero kinetic energy. This corresponds to the threshold frequency, $f_0$.

faq.q: Does a more intense light beam have more energetic photons?
faq.a: No, this is a critical misconception. The energy of each individual photon depends only on its frequency ($E=hf$). A more intense light beam of the same frequency simply means there are *more photons* arriving per unit area per second. It's like the difference between a trickle of raindrops and a downpour; the size of each drop (photon energy) is the same, but the number of drops (number of photons) is different.

sections.content: At the turn of the 20th century, physicists were puzzled by an experiment called the photoelectric effect. When light shines on a metal, it can knock electrons out. However, the way this happened defied the classical wave theory of light. This lesson delves into how Albert Einstein's revolutionary idea—that light consists of particles called photons—perfectly explained these observations and laid the foundation for quantum physics.

The classical wave theory of light predicted that the energy of a light wave was spread evenly over the wavefront and related to its intensity (brightness). This led to predictions that contradicted experimental results:

items: **Prediction vs. Reality (Threshold Frequency):** Wave theory suggested any frequency of light, if intense enough, could cause emission. Experiments showed emission only occurs above a specific 'threshold frequency', unique to each metal.

**Prediction vs. Reality (Time Delay):** Wave theory predicted a time lag for low-intensity light, as energy would need to accumulate to eject an electron. Experiments showed emission is virtually instantaneous, provided the frequency is above the threshold.

**Prediction vs. Reality (Kinetic Energy):** Wave theory linked electron kinetic energy to the light's intensity. Experiments showed that maximum kinetic energy depends only on the light's frequency, not its intensity.

sections.content: Einstein's Photon Model and the Photoelectric Equation

Einstein proposed that light energy is not continuous but quantised into discrete packets called photons. The energy of a single photon is directly proportional to its frequency. This 'all-or-nothing' interaction between one photon and one electron explains the experimental observations perfectly.

For an electron to be liberated, the energy from one incident photon, $hf$, must be sufficient to overcome the binding energy holding the electron to the metal. This minimum energy is the work function, $\Phi$. Any excess energy is transferred to the electron as kinetic energy. The maximum kinetic energy, $KE_{max}$, corresponds to the least tightly bound electrons on the surface.

items: **$hf$**: Energy of a single incident photon.
**$\Phi$**: Work function energy, the 'price' to free an electron from the metal surface.
**$KE_{max}$**: Maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectron, the 'change' left over.

sections.question: Light of wavelength 420 nm is incident on a clean caesium surface. The work function of caesium is $2.14$ eV. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons in joules. (Planck constant $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}$ J s, speed of light $c = 3.00 \times 10^8$ m s$^{-1}$, elementary charge $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19}$ C).
sections.solution:
**Step 1: Convert all quantities to SI units.**
The work function is given in electronvolts (eV). We must convert it to joules.
$\Phi = 2.14 \text{ eV} \times (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}) = 3.424 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

**Step 2: Calculate the energy of an incident photon.**
First, find the frequency $f$ from the wavelength $\lambda = 420 \text{ nm} = 420 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$.
$f = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}}{420 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} = 7.143 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$
Now, calculate the photon energy, $E = hf$.
$E = (6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}) \times (7.143 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}) = 4.736 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

**Step 3: Apply the photoelectric equation.**
$KE_{max} = hf - \Phi$
$KE_{max} = (4.736 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}) - (3.424 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J})$
$KE_{max} = 1.312 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

**Answer:** The maximum kinetic energy is $1.31 \times 10^{-19}$ J (to 3 s.f.).

sections.content: A very common mistake is failing to convert units. Work functions are often given in eV, and wavelengths in nm. Always convert to Joules and metres respectively before using them in the photoelectric equation to get an answer in Joules. Be prepared to convert your final answer back to eV if the question asks for it.

The photoelectric equation, $KE_{max} = hf - \Phi$, can be compared to the equation of a straight line, $y = mx + c$. By plotting a graph of maximum kinetic energy ($y$-axis) against frequency ($x$-axis), we can determine fundamental constants.

items: **y-intercept (c):** The negative of the work function, $-\Phi$
**x-intercept:** The threshold frequency, $f_0$. This is where $KE_{max} = 0$, so $hf_0 - \Phi = 0$, which gives $f_0 = \Phi/h$.

sections.question:
The graph shows the results of a photoelectric effect experiment for a certain metal. Use the graph to determine:
a) the threshold frequency of the metal.
b) the work function, $\Phi$, of the metal in joules.
c) a value for the Planck constant, $h$.

(The graph shows a straight line for $KE_{max}$ vs $f$. It intercepts the x-axis at $f = 4.5 \times 10^{14}$ Hz. A point on the line is ($f = 8.0 \times 10^{14}$ Hz, $KE_{max} = 2.3 \times 10^{-19}$ J).)
sections.solution:
**a) Determine the threshold frequency, $f_0$.**
The threshold frequency is the x-intercept, where the kinetic energy is zero. From the graph, this is:
$f_0 = 4.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$

**b) Determine the work function, $\Phi$.**
The work function is related to the threshold frequency by $\Phi = hf_0$. We first need to find $h$. Alternatively, we can find the y-intercept which is $-\Phi$. Let's calculate $h$ first and then find $\Phi$.

**c) Determine the Planck constant, $h$.**
The Planck constant is the gradient of the graph. We use two points to calculate the gradient. Let's use the x-intercept $(4.5 \times 10^{14}, 0)$ and the given point $(8.0 \times 10^{14}, 2.3 \times 10^{-19})$.
Gradient $h = \frac{\Delta KE_{max}}{\Delta f} = \frac{(2.3 \times 10^{-19} - 0) \text{ J}}{(8.0 \times 10^{14} - 4.5 \times 10^{14}) \text{ Hz}}$
$h = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{-19}}{3.5 \times 10^{14}} = 6.571 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$
$h \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ (to 2 s.f.)

**Back to part b):**
Now we can calculate the work function using $\Phi = hf_0$.
$\Phi = (6.571 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}) \times (4.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz})$
$\Phi = 2.957 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$
$\Phi \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$ (to 2 s.f.)

**Alternative for b):** We could extend the line to find the y-intercept. Using $y=mx+c$, we have $KE_{max} = hf - \Phi$. The y-intercept is $-\Phi$. Using the point $(8.0 \times 10^{14}, 2.3 \times 10^{-19})$ and our calculated $h$: $2.3 \times 10^{-19} = (6.571 \times 10^{-34})(8.0 \times 10^{14}) - \Phi$. This gives $2.3 \times 10^{-19} = 5.257 \times 10^{-19} - \Phi$, so $\Phi = 2.957 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.

sections.content: Questions about the effect of intensity are extremely common. Remember the mantra: 'Intensity affects quantity, frequency affects quality'. Higher intensity means more photons, so more electrons are emitted per second (higher photoelectric current). Higher frequency means each photon has more energy, so each emitted electron has a higher maximum kinetic energy.

interactiveEmbed.title: Photoelectric effect
interactiveEmbed.hint: Increase frequency and intensity — see threshold frequency and photoelectron energy (22.2).

attribution.source: PhET Interactive Simulations, University of Colorado Boulder

steps.caption: Light delivers energy in photons: E = hf (quantum model).
steps.embedHint: Increase photon energy (frequency) — each photon carries more energy.

steps.caption: Electrons are bound by work function Φ — minimum energy to escape the metal.
steps.embedHint: Read the work function for each metal target.

steps.caption: If hf > Φ, electrons are emitted with KEmax = hf − Φ.
steps.embedHint: Above threshold frequency, electrons appear — measure their maximum KE.

steps.caption: Brighter light (higher intensity) increases rate, not KEmax — classic exam trap.
steps.embedHint: Raise intensity only — more electrons, same maximum KE.
